title: systemtest - new set of BATS tests for RHEL8 gating

comments.body: Excellent first step @edsantiago !  I'd be fine with merging and adjusting with follow up PR's if the test pass and @mtrmac is hip.

In response to your wrapper questions:

>  Is anything in this repo going to invoke the tests? I’m worried that they will get silently out of sync / broken if not regularly exercised.

Complete agreement: we have a mess with podman right now because the gating tests don't run in CI, so things break often and I have to go back and run damage control. 

I would like to run these tests as part of CI. Do you know how/where I could add them?

> What is the intended relationship between this and the tests in integration/? Should this eventually replace them? Is there a reason to independently keep both long-term?

I honestly don't know. The integration tests currently (seem to?) cover more than these do, but I honestly just can't grok them nor can I figure out how to run them, let alone package them in an rpm. I think if someone finds them useful, they should remain. My goal in these tests was to have something readable, simple, so that if they fail a monkey like me can read the test, understand it, and find a clean reproducer for filing bugs. From what I see of the ginkgo tests, diagnosis can only be done by experts.

Many thanks for your thorough and helpful review.

> I would like to run these tests as part of CI. Do you know how/where I could add them?

The CI starts at `.travis.yml`, so either there, or inside the existing container (`make check` / `make test-integration`).
 
> > What is the intended relationship between this and the tests in integration/? Should this eventually replace them? Is there a reason to independently keep both long-term?
> 
> I honestly don't know. The integration tests currently (seem to?) cover more than these do, but I honestly just can't grok them nor can I figure out how to run them, let alone package them in an rpm. 

Yeah, they currently pretty strongly expect to be running inside a container (which should be reasonably possible to fix) and include building several registry servers and a two-year-old OpenShift (which _could_ be fixed but maybe shouldn’t :), for RPM tests; ideally we would maintain pre-built containers for this, and get the CI time from 20 minutes to… 5?)

As for Go vs. BATS, that rather depends on the complexity of the test code — the Go `assertSkopeoSucceeds` and the like is suspiciously close to a shell script already, with extra punctuation.

> I think if someone finds them useful, they should remain. My goal in these tests was to have something readable, simple, so that if they fail a monkey like me can read the test, understand it, and find a clean reproducer for filing bugs. From what I see of the ginkgo tests, diagnosis can only be done by experts.

I thought that that code was reasonably clear about what it was executing … though setting up a reproducer environment, even with the available `make shell`, is certainly a lot of pain.

>The CI starts at .travis.yml

AFAICT the Travis scripts only build containerized skopeo, never actually `binary-local` or any equivalent. I have no idea how to invoke the BATS tests in this scenario, so for the time being I'm giving up. Any objection to merging this as it is?

> > The CI starts at .travis.yml
> 
> AFAICT the Travis scripts only build containerized skopeo, never actually `binary-local` or any equivalent. I have no idea how to invoke the BATS tests in this scenario, so for the time being I'm giving up.

`.travis.yml`→`make check`→`make test-integration`→`make build-container`&`$(CONTAINER_RUN) hack/make.sh test-integration`→`hack/make/test-integration`→`make binary-local`&`make install`&`go test ./integration`.  Yes, it would be great to streamline this.

I don't think I spoke clearly, I just don't have the right vocabulary to do so. What I was trying to say was that the CI process never actually builds a skopeo binary on any actual host VM, it only does so inside a container. The BATS tests use podman to run a registry, and running podman-within-podman is fragile: I did not want to spend time on that. 

But I just did, spent the morning trying to get a `test-system` target running inside the container, and cannot. The most recent failure, the one I'm now giving up on, is:
```
Error: could not get runtime: kernel does not support overlay fs: 'overlay' is not supported over <unknown> at "/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay": backing file system is unsupported for this graph driver
```

@edsantiago volume mount an empty directory on /var/lib/containers.

podman run -v /tmp/tempdir123444:/var/lib/containers:Z ...

@rhatdan that then yields (after much fussing):
```
Error: could not get runtime: kernel does not support overlay fs: 'overlay' is not supported over tmpfs at "/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay": backing file system is unsupported for this graph driver
```
Changing `/etc/containers/storage.conf:driver` from overlay to vfs (before any podman ops) yields:
```
ERRO[0000] error creating network namespace for container 4cbf2226c0afad24f35c08b9581039eb1620cefe05ad8a62139f86c6a461e0bd: mount --make-rshared /var/run/netns failed: "operation not permitted" 
Error: failed to mount shm tmpfs "/var/lib/containers/storage/vfs-containers/4cbf2226c0afad24f35c08b9581039eb1620cefe05ad8a62139f86c6a461e0bd/userdata/shm": operation not permitted
```
(adding `--net=host` resolves the first ERRO message but not the failed-to-mount one).

This is why I was reluctant to dive into podman-in-podman...
@edsantiago Use /var/tmp not /tmp.  But this would require a --privileged container anyways.  Podman will not be able to run within a locked down podman container.

Using `/var/tmp`, running as root with `--privileged`, hand-editing `/etc/containers/storage.conf` to change overlay to vfs, removing the `mountopt` flag, adding `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs` to all podman invocations. With that I can finally get podman-in-podman to work without error, on simple stuff, but networking doesn't seem to work: all skopeo copy commands fail with:
```
time="2019-05-28T14:22:45Z" level=fatal msg="Error creating an updated image manifest: Get https://production.cloudflare.docker.com/registry-v2/docker/registry/v2/blobs/sha256/64/64f5d945efcc0f39ab11b3cd4ba403cc9fefe1fa3613123ca016cf3708e8cafb/data?verify=1559056335-YjM3xM0QCt3jPGWIaQQz%2FthEZTQ%3D: dial tcp: i/o timeout"
```
or
```
time="2019-05-28T14:22:07Z" level=fatal msg="Error writing blob: read tcp 10.88.9.30:35970->104.18.121.25:443: read: connection reset by peer" 
```
or
```
time="2019-05-28T14:23:20Z" level=fatal msg="Error initializing source docker://busybox:latest: Get https://auth.docker.io/token?scope=repository%3Alibrary%2Fbusybox%3Apull&service=registry.docker.io: net/http: TLS handshake timeout"
```
I feel like I'm in diminishing-returns territory: spending days of effort without any actual progress. Is there any possible way to get this CI to run without the multiple levels of podman-in-podman?

Rebased, and added one more commit which (on my setup) addresses the podman-in-podman problems while still allowing it to work on a regular setup (i.e. for gating tests). The tests should now run as part of the in-progress-as-i-write-this CI.

CI ran new tests, and they passed, but output looks weird; new force-push invokes bats with `--tap` to see if that fixes the output. There's also this weirdness when cleaning up the tempdir:
```
rm: cannot remove ‘/var/tmp/podman-tmp.eVP6m2/storage’: Permission denied
rm: cannot remove ‘/var/tmp/podman-tmp.eVP6m2/cache’: Permission denied
```
`--tap` fixed the broken output. EPERM issue remains; I'm not going to spend any time on it unless requested to.
Ping, is there anything more needed on this? I'd like to move on to the actual gating-test aspect.
